coverage_note: This is a broad L1-L4+ driver map for the national NL housing decision. It covers prices/transactions, supply, grid, nitrogen, financing, mortgage standards, wealth/liquidity, Box 3/rental policy, demographics, macro/labour, regional and personal-fit channels. It is not an infinite-web exhaustive crawl; further work should branch by city, price band, property type and buyer profile.

provenance_policy.direct_cala_tag_rule: Only nested source objects extracted from raw Cala responses may carry source_origin=cala or source:cala.
provenance_policy.factor_tag_rule: Factor nodes carry derived_from_source_origins and derived-from:* UI tags, never direct Cala source tags.

augmentation.note: Analyst gap-scan nodes + annotated causal/confounder/feedback/conditioning edges merged additively. Canonical def: reference/nl-housing-factor-tree.md.

edges.mechanism: Long yields set 10y+ fixed mortgage pricing
ECB/inflation policy feeds through to mortgage rates
Higher rates cut borrowing capacity / bid ceilings
HRA raises effective affordability
Nibud norms set capacity (wage +4.1% 2026)
DEMAND CHANNEL (S1/S3): capacity sets bid ceilings
THE CONTESTED EDGE: rate->price is graph_not_settled. Real via borrowing_capacity in S1/S3; absent (confounded) in S2.
S2 latent common cause: risk-off regime moves rates
S2 latent common cause: risk-off regime moves prices (why rate->price is unsettled)
SUPPLY CHANNEL (S3): shortage lifts prices
Nitrogen/grid/viability/execution throttle the pipeline -> shortage
Permitting blocks completions
Connection queues delay/raise cost of projects
Box3/tax pushes private landlords to sell
Affordable Rent Act caps rents -> landlord exit. TWO-SIDED + reversal watch.
edges.reversal_watch: cabinet plans to ease rent controls

edges.mechanism: INVESTOR_WINDOW: ex-rental supply eases owner-occupied prices
Fewer private rentals -> rental scarcity (the other side)
Wages/employment set real income & capacity
Household formation -> demand -> price
Wealth/gifts -> down-payment capacity (WEAK / unquantified)
REGIONAL_DIVERGENCE: local tightness modulates price
Arrears/defaults tighten credit & financial-stability buffers
Price momentum feeds expectations
Self-fulfilling demand (FOMO) moves price
Bank forecasts (1.5-3.1%) shape expectations
User vars re-weight this scenario FOR them (PRD 15: never mutates market support)
User vars re-weight this scenario FOR them (PRD 15: never mutates market support)

factors.label: Financing pressure
factors.summary: Below is a concise overview of key 2025–2026 Dutch mortgage data sources and the indicators they track, focused on DNB, AFM, ECB, HDN, Kadaster, DMFCO and related mortgage market reports.
top_metrics: Below is a concise overview of key 2025–2026 Dutch mortgage data sources and the indicators they track, focused on DNB, AFM, ECB, HDN, Kadaster, DMFCO and related mortgage market reports.

**Household mortgage debt (bank lending)** | Outstanding residential mortgage loans by banks: €611bn in June 2025 (+5.2% YoY; +2.4% YoY in June 2024). | [id: ab507044-f6c3-4bbf-897e-553609d820be], [id: df8a0f9d-1c60-4b80-ae43-63d6aa87a2ec], [id: 38be9fb2-4015...

**Macroprudential policy** | Minimum floor on risk weights for Dutch mortgage loans (in place Jan 2022) expires 30 Nov 2026. | [id: ab507044-f6c3-4bbf-897e-553609d820be], [id: 734095da-5185-44c0-9cc9-466fa0bdf9ca]
## 3. European Central Bank (ECB)

**Wage growth (affects affordability)** | ECB wage tracker with headline and excluding-one-off-payments indicators, with quarterly updates through Q4 2026 (e.g., Q4 2026 headline 2.7%, excluding one-off 2.7%). | [id: 78dedbe0-908f-4d70-b2a9-a002ea12159f], [id...

**Policy rates** | ECB rate influences tracker mortgages; ECB refinancing rate cut of 0.25% announced 5 June 2025 affects tracker mortgage rates. | [id: 20008dee-e9e6-4c05-a66e-8d1e1f8c10a2], [id: 4776c15d-23b6-41e4-a2ad-3621eb27ce4c]

**Mortgage applications** | Near-comprehensive coverage of Dutch mortgage applications:
• 2023 → 2024: 483,922 applications in 2024 (+29% vs 2023).
• 2025: 563,000–564,000 applications (+12% vs 2024).
• 2026: Jan 36,412 (–17% YoY); Q1 +23% YoY...

**Application mix (purchase vs refinance)** | May 2025: 48,928 total; 30,525 (62.3%) for home purchase (record for May). Q4 2025: ~147,800 applications (+10.2% QoQ). | [id: 0ace8677-982d-4c30-a013-091292251c63], [id: 5af83ec4-db2f-4224-82df-683d860b899f]

factors.label: Structural shortage
factors.summary: Dutch Housing Shortage (ABF Research Primos, latest 2025–2026)
top_metrics: ## Dutch Housing Shortage (ABF Research Primos, latest 2025–2026)
**Shortage (absolute)** | 390,000–410,000 homes in 2023–2026 | 410,000 in early 2026 (up from 400,000 in 2025); 401,000 in another 2025 tally; 390,000 in 2023
**Projected peak** | Until at least 2028 | ABF now expects improvement no earlier than 2028 (previous forecasts pointed to 2026)
**Projected shortage by 2038** | ~205,000 homes | Long-term ABF outlook
**Normal market tension (government goal)** | 2% by 2031 | Previous and current governments aim for a 2% shortage (earlier target was 2.1% by 2031)
**Homes needed to hit 2% now** | ~226,000–232,000 | Varies by estimate; 226,000 in 2025, 232,000 in 2026
**ABF’s 2030 need estimate** | 981,000 homes by 2030 | To accommodate household formation given current trends

factors.summary: Evidence of Dutch Grid Congestion Delaying Housing Construction (2025–2026)
top_metrics: ## Evidence of Dutch Grid Congestion Delaying Housing Construction (2025–2026)

**G4 cities**: Amsterdam, Rotterdam, The Hague (Den Haag), and Utrecht warned in October 2025 that grid congestion threatens the construction of about **160,000 homes** in their municipalities; the city aldermen wrote to the national minister warning that hou...

**Amsterdam**: From 1 July 2026, households and small businesses seeking new or upgraded electricity connections will face a waiting list due to grid congestion. Liander and TenneT are undertaking large‑scale grid expansion (2,600 substations, 39 stations) ex...

**The Hague**: The municipality warned in 2026 that new national priority rules (effective 1 July 2026) would affect housing, education and businesses; housing projects and schools can apply early from 1 October 2026 to reserve a place on the waiting list [id...

**Rotterdam region**: Stedin and TenneT are strengthening the grid; a prioritisation framework effective 1 July 2026 places residential construction, hospitals, emergency services, schools and public transport in priority groups for new connections [id: 3576f...

**Almere**: In 2025 it became known that not only new businesses and social facilities must wait until 2029 for grid connections, but new homes may soon become unconnectable due to congestion [id: 1e2d8c86-1caa-40cc-9d57-633fb1759a04; id: 613310e5-25e8-4def-9...

**Flevopolder, Gelderland, Utrecht (FGU region)**: TenneT reported the high‑voltage grid in these provinces reached maximum capacity; from 1 July 2026 a temporary connection pause for new or heavier connections is in effect. In parts of the Utrecht region no...
**Noord‑Veluwe**: Construction of about **7,500 homes** was delayed due to an overloaded electricity grid [id: 1e2d8c86-1caa-40cc-9d57-633fb1759a04].

factors.label: Regional tightness
factors.summary: Netherlands Housing Market Data 2025–2026: Key Indicators & Recent Figures
top_metrics: ## Netherlands Housing Market Data 2025–2026: Key Indicators & Recent Figures
The Dutch housing shortage indicator stood at **−4.8%** as of Q2 2025, reflecting a persistent structural deficit.
The Netherlands Construction Confidence Indicator was **18.10%** in April 2026 (down from 22.10% previously), according to Eurostat — still among the highest in Europe.
The government has planned **150,000 new homes by 2030** to address the shortage, but regulatory barriers continue to slow new construction.

factors.label: Macro demand
factors.summary: Netherlands 2025–2026: Macro & Demographic Indicators Driving Housing Demand and House Prices
top_metrics: # Netherlands 2025–2026: Macro & Demographic Indicators Driving Housing Demand and House Prices
**Negotiated wages rose 5% in full-year 2025** (CBS). In Q1 2026, negotiated wages were up **4.5%** year-on-year (CBS).

DNB (December 2025 Autumn Projections) projected negotiated wage growth in the private sector to decline gradually to an average of **3.7% in 2025** and fall further to **2.9% in 2026**, after 6.0% in 2024.
Real disposable household income increased by **4.5% in 2024** as wage growth exceeded inflation (DNB).
**May 2026: 3.9%** (399,000 unemployed), down from previous months (CBS).
Unemployment was **4.1% in February 2026** (CBS).
The Netherlands Consumer Confidence Index stood at **−24 in February 2026** (CBS/Stooq).
CBS reported "the second-largest drop ever seen in consumer confidence" in early 2026, and confidence "continues to decline" through mid-2026.

factors.label: Borrowing capacity
factors.summary: Dutch home‑buyer borrowing capacity 2025–2026: subdriver breakdown
top_metrics: # Dutch home‑buyer borrowing capacity 2025–2026: subdriver breakdown

**2025–2026 trend:** Rates have been on the rise, with economists noting that rising mortgage rates mean buyers have higher monthly payments and that stricter lending may be warranted if inflation/energy prices stay high.
**NHG rate benefit:** Mortgages qualifying for the National Mortgage Guarantee (NHG) typically receive 0.2–0.3 percentage points lower interest rates.
**Example rate:** One 2026 illustration shows a 2.05% interest rate in a sample calculation.
**2026 change:** No explicit rate cap is specified, but rate levels feed directly into Nibud’s affordability calculations and the 2026 lending‑norm recalibration.
**2025 benchmark:** Maximum mortgage is set at 4.25 × gross annual income. Single buyers receive an additional €17,000 supplement.
**2026 wage growth:** Nibud expects around 4% wage growth; another source cites 4.1% expected wage growth.
**2026 borrowing effect:** A household earning €70,000 is expected to borrow about €6,000 more in 2026 due to wage growth. Higher incomes gain more in euros than lower incomes.

factors.summary: Netherlands Housing Market Tightness (2025–2026): Subdriver Breakdown
top_metrics: ## Netherlands Housing Market Tightness (2025–2026): Subdriver Breakdown
Subdriver | Latest 2025–2026 figures | Notes/Source

**Active listings** | Q4 2025: ~49,500 new listings in the quarter (record high for Q4); total supply under 30,000 at quarter-end, up 16% YoY. Q1 2026: 29,922 homes for sale (NVM), up 20.6% YoY. | Supply growth mainly from former landlords selling; market sti...

**NVM tightness indicator** | Q4 2025: 1.9 (down from 2.3 earlier in 2025). Q1 2026: 2.6. Q2 2025: 2.3. Q1 2025: 2.3. Reference: balanced market = 5–10; below 5 = seller’s/tight market. | Indicator rose in Q1 2026 but remains in tight territory.

**Viewings per property** | Q4 2025: average 7.5 actual viewings per home, down from 10.4 a year earlier. Q3 2025: ~8 viewings/month in big cities. Q1 2026: viewings down 7.9% vs prior quarter. | More supply spreads demand, reducing average viewings.
**Bids per property** | Q4 2025: average 2.8 bids per home, down from 3.7 a year earlier. | Reflects reduced competition amid rising listings.

**Overbidding share** | Q4 2025: 72% of homes sold above asking price. Q1 2026: 71.4% (some sources note two‑thirds). Examples: Utrecht Q1 2026 77%; Almere Q1 2026 86.7%. | Majority still overbid, but the share is edging down.

**Sale‑to‑asking ratio (average premium)** | Q4 2025: average 5.2% above asking. Q1 2026: average 4.7% above asking. Q1 2025: average 3.7% above asking. Regional example: Flevoland Q4 2025 average +6.7%. | Premiums are moderating as competition eases slightly.

factors.summary: Decision Framework: Buy Now vs. Wait — Netherlands Home Buyer (2025–2026)
top_metrics: # Decision Framework: Buy Now vs. Wait — Netherlands Home Buyer (2025–2026)

**Budget** | Total savings available for purchase plus all closing costs (cash on hand). | • Average home prices: €467,355 in October 2024; prices were 11.5% higher YoY [id: 9aa06c64-1f7f-4ef7-a474-4f14a14a2770].
• Typical closing costs: 5–10% of purchase...

**Down Payment** | Cash available to put toward purchase and closing costs. | • Maximum LTV is 100% of appraisal value; additional costs must be financed yourself [id: 95b56800-ea80-48be-a009-edcde8834049].
• First-time buyers under 35: exempt from transf...

**Monthly Ceiling** | Maximum monthly housing payment you can sustain (mortgage + taxes + insurance + VVE/reserve). | • Mortgage interest rates (approximate levels in 2024–2025): 10-year ~4.08–4.49%; 20-year ~4.31–4.66%; 30-year ~4.33–4.73% [id: 4caa5b34-a134...

factors.label: Rate-price split test
factors.summary: To test whether Dutch mortgage rates affect house prices through borrowing capacity (i.e., check the conditional independence `mortgage_rate ⟂ house_price | borrowing_capacity`), you would need a matched time-series dataset with the following observable variables, sources, and considerations for 2025–2026:
top_metrics: **Mortgage rate** | Weighted average interest rate on new mortgage loans; rates by fixation period | DNB (De Nederlandsche Bank) publishes mortgage rates from 2003 onward; DNB tables also used by Statistics Netherlands for charts | Monthly (DNB publishes weig...

**House price** | Price index of existing owner-occupied dwellings (e.g., 2020=100 or 2015=100); average transaction price | Statistics Netherlands (CBS) + Kadaster (Land Registry) joint HPI (SPAR-based); monthly and quarterly tables (e.g., “Existing own home...
**Household income / wages** | Average gross household income; wage growth rates | CBS wage/income series; CPB/NIBUD wage growth forecasts (e.g., +4.3% 2025, +4.1% 2026 mentioned) | Monthly
**Household income/wages**: primary driver of borrowing capacity alongside rates; NIBUD explicitly links wage growth to higher borrowing power in 2025–2026.
**NHG parameters and eligibility**: NHG limit changes (e.g., €470,000 from January 2026) affect borrowing capacity via cost and risk, and are used in affordability calculators.

**Temporal alignment**: Mortgage rates lead borrowing capacity almost immediately, but house prices adjust more gradually; include lags (e.g., 1–6 months) in the model to capture transmission delays. Borrowing capacity can be constructed each month by applyin...
## Limitations for 2025–2026
**Sample size for 2025–2026** | Only 24 monthly observations; statistical power will be limited, especially for nonlinear or lagged effects.

House Price Index: +8.57% YoY in 2025, slowing to +4.98% YoY in March 2026
Average WOZ value: €439,000 in 2026 (+10.3% YoY)
Existing homes sold: 238,695 in 2025 (+15.61% YoY)
New-build completions: 69,189 in 2025; 13,711 in Q1 2026 (−11.6% YoY)
Average existing-home price growth: +5.0% YoY in March 2026
GDP growth: +0.1% in Q1 2026 (quarter-on-quarter)
Unemployment: 3.9% in May 2026

factors.label: Transfer tax and NHG
factors.summary: Netherlands 2026 Property Transfer Tax & NHG Mortgage Limit
top_metrics: ## Netherlands 2026 Property Transfer Tax & NHG Mortgage Limit
There is some discrepancy among sources regarding the exact 2026 threshold:
**Several sources** (including ABN AMRO, Independer, Bijbrut, and Rekentools) state the exemption threshold is **€555,000** for 2026.
**Other sources** (DutchTaxGuide, Financieren.nl) report the threshold as **€510,000** for 2026.
**Conditions for the exemption (0% transfer tax):**

> **Note:** The discrepancy likely reflects a legislative change during 2026. The most recent Dutch-language sources (ABN AMRO, Independer, Rekentools) indicate the threshold was raised to **€555,000** in 2026.
### Standard Transfer Tax Rates (Overdrachtsbelasting) 2026
First-time buyer (startersvrijstelling) | **0%** (if conditions met)

factors.label: Rental regulation and Box 3
factors.summary: **2025–2026 Dutch rental regulation and Box 3 tax changes affecting buy‑to‑let investors who sell homes**
top_metrics: **2025–2026 Dutch rental regulation and Box 3 tax changes affecting buy‑to‑let investors who sell homes**

Effective 1 July 2024; expands rent regulation from the social sector into the mid‑segment (middensegment). Rents are now capped via the Woningwaarderingsstelsel (WWS) point‑system for homes up to 186 points, creating a regulated mid‑rent segment between the...
Maximum rent levels (2026 reference): low‑segment threshold at €859 (135 points); mid‑segment up to €1,228 (144–186 points) [id: 3cf3f153-874b-40ef-afb9-7c78030887e0].

Annual rent increase caps (regulated low and mid segments): wage growth +0.5% (mid‑segment) or +1% (as cited in some summaries) [id: 3147e990-9216-4e1f-bc98-27248225a71a][id: c2ab9a85-e088-43f2-836e-40f1bc2858f3].

**Enforcement from 1 Jan 2025:** municipalities may impose administrative fines on landlords who exceed the WWS‑based maximum rent [id: 3076340d-c5b4-460e-9625-6285a2cfb441][id: 8f44ee03-6db4-4685-a163-fd7c3b27936d][id: 03be714a-27c1-45f6-a09e-c32da9a23909].

**Existing contracts:** For tenancies that started before 1 July 2024 where the property scores ≤143 points and the rent is effectively in the regulated sector, landlords must reduce the rent to the WWS maximum by 1 July 2025; tenants can initiate proceedings...

**New contracts:** As of 1 July 2024, landlords must attach a WWS point‑calculation; if the asked rent exceeds the WWS‑based maximum, the tenant can seek an immediate reduction via the Huurcommissie or municipality [id: 210b2ec1-cf16-4295-acf3-8db9836d4d1e][i...

Government estimates indicate over 300,000 homes could see average rent reductions of around €190 per month for new tenants [id: 0e9d7f09-d7b1-4bcb-996a-ed18c2bd4c1b][id: b0a84be1-f3a4-4b4a-941a-31e8802d39c2].

factors.summary: **Dutch mortgage borrowing capacity and Nibud lending standards – key changes for 2026**
top_metrics: **Dutch mortgage borrowing capacity and Nibud lending standards – key changes for 2026**
Area | What changes in 2026 | Effect on households

**Max mortgage (overall)** | Most households can borrow **slightly more** than in 2025. Nibud assumes an average wage increase of **4.1%**, which lifts borrowing capacity for many. However, the financing‑last‑percentages (share of income that may go to mortga...

**Income assumptions used in norms** | Nibud bases the 2026 norms on an expected **average wage increase of 4.1%**. At the same time, the financing‑last‑percentages are **reduced slightly** to account for higher living‑cost inflation, limiting the share of in...

**Student debt (studieschuld)** | Student debt continues to be calculated based on the **actual monthly payment** (termijnbedrag) since 2024, and the weighting is **tightened** for 2026 – meaning the debt counts **more heavily** in the mortgage assessment. Ba...
**NHG limit** | The standard NHG (Nationale Hypotheek Garantie) cost limit rises from **€450,000 to €470,000**. | Mortgages up to €470,000 can qualify for NHG in 2026 (subject to other conditions).

factors.label: Energy label mortgage capacity
factors.summary: **Dutch home energy labels (energielabel) affect mortgages and buyer behaviour in 2025–2026 through four main mechanisms: borrowing capacity, interest‑rate discounts, renovation‑loan top‑ups, and buyer demand. Measurable rules and key sources are summarised below.**
top_metrics: **Dutch home energy labels (energielabel) affect mortgages and buyer behaviour in 2025–2026 through four main mechanisms: borrowing capacity, interest‑rate discounts, renovation‑loan top‑ups, and buyer demand. Measurable rules and key sources are summarised b...
### 1. Mortgage borrowing capacity (2025–2026)

Since 2024, the National Institute for Family Finance Information (Nibud) ties the maximum mortgage to a home’s energy label. Better labels increase the amount you can borrow for purchase; lower labels unlock extra funds for energy‑saving improvements.
**2025 extra borrowing (purchase price) by label cluster** (on top of the standard income‑based limit):
**2025 extra borrowing for energy‑saving measures** (if the loan is used for specified improvements):
**2026 changes – reduced top‑ups for the highest labels**:
For energy‑saving measures, label A+++ goes from €10,000 (2025) to €0 (2026).
**2025**: NHG covers mortgages up to €450,000; if energy‑saving measures are included, you can borrow an additional 6% (up to €477,000).

factors.label: Nitrogen construction constraint
factors.summary: Evidence that nitrogen rules are delaying Dutch housing construction (2025–2026)
top_metrics: ## Evidence that nitrogen rules are delaying Dutch housing construction (2025–2026)

**244,000 homes** (more than one‑third of the 631,000 homes planned through 2030) are at risk of not being built due to nitrogen‑based pollution rules, according to the construction lobby group Bouwend Nederland. Around 75% of planned non‑residential projects...

**500,000 homes** could stall in the coming years, primarily because of the nitrogen impasse and an overburdened electricity network, warns the Interprovincial Consultation (IPO). This represents at least half of the remaining housing target toward 1 million...
ING forecasts only **68,000 new‑build homes in 2026**, citing structural bottlenecks including nitrogen‑related permitting requirements and grid congestion.
**Natura 2000 sites nationwide**: Over half of new housing projects are located within five kilometers of nitrogen‑sensitive nature reserves, making permits extremely difficult to obtain.

**Noord‑Brabant**: The province temporarily halted granting permits for infrastructure, agriculture, industry, housing or sustainability projects if they would add nitrogen to Natura 2000 areas, citing a risk of “total impoverishment of nature.”

**Nature permits now required for every project**: A Council of State ruling (the Porthos case) invalidated the previous “building exemption,” meaning a nature permit must now be sought for each construction project. Large housing projects near Natura 2000 si...

**Permit complexity and uncertainty**: New building projects must prove they will not increase nitrogen emissions, a requirement that varies widely across municipalities. The government’s plan to raise the nitrogen threshold in January 2026 is under legal rev...

factors.label: City overbidding
factors.summary: Based on the provided context, here is the 2025–2026 data available for the requested metrics in Amsterdam, Utrecht, Rotterdam, The Hague, and Eindhoven. Where city‑specific figures are not provided in the context, national (Netherlands) figures are noted instead.
top_metrics: Based on the provided context, here is the 2025–2026 data available for the requested metrics in Amsterdam, Utrecht, Rotterdam, The Hague, and Eindhoven. Where city‑specific figures are not provided in the context, national (Netherlands) figures are noted ins...
**National trend (Q1 2025)**: About two‑thirds of homes sold above asking price; average overbid approximately 3.7% above asking.

**National trend (Q2 2025)**: Overbidding remained standard practice; the proportion of homes sold above asking price reached nearly 74%, the highest level in recent periods. Average bid was 4.1% above asking in Q2 (versus 2.6% in Q1).
**Amsterdam**: Buyers paid on average **7% above asking price**. In the Oost district, **87% of homes sold above asking price**.

**Sale‑to‑asking interpretation**: The percentages of homes sold above asking (e.g., 74% nationally, 87% in Amsterdam Oost) and average overbids (3.7–7%) together indicate the sale‑to‑asking ratio; no separate city‑by‑city ratios are provided.
**National (Q2 2025)**: NVM market tightness indicator **2.3** (number of homes available per buyer; lower values indicate tighter supply).
**National (Q2 2024)**: **2.4** (for comparison).
**Amsterdam (Q1 2025)**: Tightness indicator for the municipality increased to **3.0** (versus 2.0 in Q4 2024). For Greater Amsterdam apartments, the indicator was **2.7**.

factors.label: Migration and household formation
factors.summary: Below is a summary of key Netherlands data and Statistics Netherlands (CBS) figures and forecasts that track migration, population growth, household formation, household size, and housing demand, with a focus on 2025–2026 where available.
top_metrics: Below is a summary of key Netherlands data and Statistics Netherlands (CBS) figures and forecasts that track migration, population growth, household formation, household size, and housing demand, with a focus on 2025–2026 where available.

**2025 (full year):** Immigration 306,549; emigration 211,863; net migration 94,686. Net migration has been positive since 2008; between 2003–2007 it was negative. [id: d989a5ad-d666-430b-be6f-deae38b3b705]

**Q1 2025:** Immigration 79,000; emigration 49,000; net migration 30,600 (higher than Q1 2024’s 27,100). Population growth in Q1 2025 was due entirely to net migration, as deaths outnumbered births. [id: 841b18b6-cb4f-4151-9632-6f4eb9bb23a2], [id: 19527d9c-4e...

**Q1 2026:** Population grew by nearly 10,000 to reach 18.14 million. Growth was lower than in recent years due to lower immigration; Q1 2026 growth was the lowest since 2015. [id: 22036dbf-02a1-4196-8547-a20943f42415]

**2025:** Population grew by 87,000 to reach 18.13 million. Growth slowed for the third consecutive year and was driven entirely by migration, as deaths (≈173,000) outnumbered births (≈166,000). [id: d8818411-7834-480e-94e3-c05f9f0f485d], [id: 04b8fdd0-a1e9-4...
**First three quarters 2025:** Growth of 77,500. [id: d8818411-7834-480e-94e3-c05f9f0f485d]

factors.label: Credit risk and arrears
factors.summary: Below is a concise guide to Netherlands data (2025–2026) that tracks mortgage arrears, payment stress, household debt risk, defaults, negative‑equity (underwater) exposure, and broader financial‑stability risks for homeowners, focusing on sources you asked about (DNB, AFM, ECB/EBA, BKR, banking/sector reports).
top_metrics: Below is a concise guide to Netherlands data (2025–2026) that tracks mortgage arrears, payment stress, household debt risk, defaults, negative‑equity (underwater) exposure, and broader financial‑stability risks for homeowners, focusing on sources you asked ab...
## Key sources and what they track (2025–2026)

**Household debt risk (LTV, LTI, debt‑to‑GDP)** | DNB loan‑level mortgage data; DNB/AFM monitor; Statista series | DNB collects granular loan‑level data covering ~87% of Dutch mortgage loans and reports on high LTV/LTI use, household indebtedness (mortgage de...

**Financial‑stability risks for homeowners** | DNB Financial Stability Report (Spring & Autumn 2025); DNB Financial Stability Outlook 2026; EBA Opinion on Dutch housing market; ECB references | DNB’s semi‑annual Financial Stability Reports and the 2026 outloo...
## Brief highlights from 2025–2026 publications

**DNB/AFM Monitor on Mortgage Lending Standards and Financial Stability 2026** – First joint annual monitor; uses DNB’s loan‑level dataset to report on LTV/LTI levels, high LTV share, arrears‑and‑default‑management, and credit‑underwriting standards across ba...

**DNB granular mortgage data (Q2 2025)** – Covers ~87% of Dutch mortgage loans; shows mortgage debt >5% YoY growth, total household mortgage debt around €868 bn, and debt‑to‑GDP near 91%. Used to gauge household vulnerability and stress‑test scenarios.

**Financial Stability Report (Spring/Autumn 2025) & Outlook 2026** – Semi‑annual DNB publications assessing systemic risks to Dutch financial stability, including high household mortgage debt, house‑price corrections, and external shocks.

factors.label: Listing portal signals
factors.summary: Below is a summary of 2025–2026 Netherlands housing market data from Funda, NVM, Huispedia, and regional reports, organized by your requested metrics.
top_metrics: Below is a summary of 2025–2026 Netherlands housing market data from Funda, NVM, Huispedia, and regional reports, organized by your requested metrics.

**Funda (2025):** 263,959 homes were listed, up 20% from 2024. Of these, 195,392 were sold through the platform. The average asking price per square meter rose 6% to €4,624. Noord-Holland commanded the highest price per square meter at €6,201. [id: 477369ff-f...
**NVM Q1 2025:** Approximately 37,000 homes were listed via NVM agents, up 22% from the prior year. [id: 9b9f7299-b877-4e89-a251-7dded0c1d84d]
**NVM Q3 2025:** Housing supply was 6.3% higher than a year earlier. [id: e975f471-e783-4e9b-9520-1877ef4d9ac9]

**NVM Q4 2025:** Supply was 16% higher than a year earlier, with the largest increases in the €400,000–€500,000 and €500,000–€750,000 price bands. The average asking price was €441,000. [id: 2eb44d66-4e57-49d3-be23-3a8d862cee02][id: e975f471-e783-4e9b-9520-18...

**NVM Q1 2026:** Supply reached around 30,000 properties (more than 20% higher than a year earlier) and around 18,200 homes in supply (the highest since 2016). The number of sales fell 27% to approximately 34,600 homes. [id: a44657f2-f0b4-4c2d-8c9a-572846eee7...
**Huispedia Q4 2025:** 12.8% of homes on the market had price reductions. [id: c1a2c5cb-a19b-4c18-ae29-f3cead25f439]

**Huispedia Q1 2026:** 12.2% of homes on the market had price reductions—still unusually high for the season. By comparison, in Q1 2025 price reductions affected about 1 in 10 homes; in Q1 2026 it was about 1 in 8 homes. [id: c1a2c5cb-a19b-4c18-ae29-f3cead25f...

factors.label: Municipal and land policy
factors.summary: Netherlands 2025–2026 Municipal & Land-Policy Factors Affecting Housing Construction
top_metrics: ## Netherlands 2025–2026 Municipal & Land-Policy Factors Affecting Housing Construction

The Dutch national government, provinces, municipalities, and housing corporations have signed **regional housing deals (woondeals)** committing to large-scale construction targets through 2030. The national ambition is approximately **900,000 new homes by 20...
**Province of Utrecht example:** Three woondeals (U10, Amersfoort, Foodvalley) commit to **83,500 new homes through 2030** — 61,000 in U10, 27,000 in Amersfoort, and 25,000 in Foodvalley.
**Province of Drenthe:** Woondeal target of at least **16,200 new homes** through 2030.
From **2025 onward**, the target is that **two-thirds (67%) of all new housing** falls in the "affordable" segment. This includes:
Within this, a minimum of **30% of new construction must be social rental housing**.
A progress report on the woondeals shows that currently only about **50% of realized housing is affordable**, falling short of the 67% ambition.

From **2026 through 2030**, municipalities receive **€7,000 per affordable home** that starts construction (for starts in 2025–2029). This grant is freely spendable to resolve local bottlenecks in area development.

factors.label: Supply bottleneck breakdown
factors.summary: Supply remains the main upside-price constraint: the existing Cala tree captures the national shortage and project pipeline, while web evidence shows the operative bottlenecks are grid capacity, nitrogen permitting, project viability and execution capacity.
factors.mechanism: If completions stay below household formation and demand, the shortage persists and prices/rents remain supported.

factors.label: Nitrogen permitting outlook
factors.summary: Nitrogen is a direct permitting risk for housing projects near sensitive nature areas. It slows or blocks supply even when demand and prices support building.
factors.mechanism: Permitting uncertainty raises delay risk and feasibility costs; fewer timely completions keep market tight.

factors.label: Mortgage lending standards outlook
factors.summary: Dutch households remain highly leveraged by European standards. Lending standards, LTV/LTI usage and NHG/Nibud rules determine how much demand converts into bids.
factors.mechanism: If standards tighten, demand is capped; if standards or guarantees become more generous, more capacity may feed into prices.

factors.label: Equity market wealth and liquidity channel

factors.mechanism: Higher liquid wealth can raise effective purchasing power beyond mortgage income tests; losses can reduce confidence and down-payment capacity.

factors.label: Box 3 and private rental tax channel
factors.summary: Box 3 taxation and rent regulation affect whether small landlords hold, sell or buy rental homes. Sales can add transaction supply for owner-occupiers while reducing rental supply.
factors.mechanism: If after-tax rental returns worsen, landlords sell; owner-occupied supply rises temporarily but rental scarcity can worsen.

factors.label: Affordable rent and landlord exit
factors.summary: Rent caps and fiscal pressure change the investor-owner calculus. The immediate owner-occupier effect can be more listings; the rental effect can be tighter supply.
factors.mechanism: Policy-driven investor sales shift tenure and can temporarily lift for-sale transactions without solving the structural shortage.

factors.label: Macro, labor and confidence
factors.summary: Macro conditions influence housing through employment, wages, confidence, inflation and rate expectations. DNB's 2026 update is softer growth with still-positive wage support.
factors.mechanism: A weaker economy can reduce confidence and hiring, while wages and inflation shape nominal borrowing capacity and policy rates.

factors.label: Personal decision layer
factors.summary: User-input + deterministic personal-exposure head (PRD 11/15). Conditions which candidate market-states weigh for THIS user; never mutates market support.
factors.mechanism: conditioning (not causal): re-weights scenarios per user via personal_fit
top_metrics: Inputs: budget, down payment, monthly ceiling, quoted rate, income/stability, move deadline, horizon, risk tolerance, FTB status, region flexibility, rent alternative.
Fixes the previously dangling parent reference for personal_decision_subdrivers.

provenance.note: Gap-scan node; canonical def reference/nl-housing-factor-tree.md. Merged additively by the generator.

factors.label: Macro regime / risk-off confounder
factors.summary: Latent common cause linking mortgage rates and house prices (structured-tier S2_growth_confounder). The reason rate->price is graph_not_settled.
factors.mechanism: confounder: risk-off / growth regime moves BOTH rates and prices; no direct rate->price edge under S2
top_metrics: Observable proxies: eurozone GDP downgrade to 0.8% (from 1.2%); energy/geopolitical shock; political-regime uncertainty (coalition collapse 3 Jun 2025).
Measuring a regime proxy is what would let the splitting test resolve rate->price.

sources.label: OECD Mar 2026: eurozone GDP cut to 0.8% (energy/Strait of Hormuz)

provenance.note: Gap-scan node; canonical def reference/nl-housing-factor-tree.md. Merged additively by the generator.

factors.label: Expectations / reflexivity loop
factors.summary: Self-reinforcing feedback: price momentum and bank forecasts shape buyer/seller expectations, which feed demand, which moves price.
factors.mechanism: feedback (loop): momentum -> expectations -> demand -> price -> momentum
top_metrics: FOMO / fear-of-further-rises amplifies upswings; cooling expectations amplify downswings.
Fed by forecast_dispersion (ING1.5 / ABN3 / Rabo3.1, 2026).

provenance.note: Gap-scan node; canonical def reference/nl-housing-factor-tree.md. Merged additively by the generator.

factors.label: House-price forecast dispersion
factors.summary: The visible contested expectation band for 2026 - the spread across major bank forecasts is itself the market contestedness.
factors.mechanism: expectation signal feeding reflexivity; wide spread = high disagreement
top_metrics: 2026 forecasts: ING ~+1.5% / ABN AMRO +3% / Rabobank +3.1% (revised down from +4.8% in Dec 2025).
Drivers cited: ex-rental supply + construction vs higher rates + cautious sentiment.

sources.label: ABN AMRO: house prices to rise ~3% in 2026 (+4% 2027)
Rabobank: ~+3.1% 2026 (cut from +4.8% Dec 2025); ING most cautious ~+1.5%

provenance.note: Gap-scan node; canonical def reference/nl-housing-factor-tree.md. Merged additively by the generator.

factors.label: Bond / swap (long) yields
factors.summary: Long-term capital-market yields that drive 10y+ fixed mortgage rates beyond the ECB policy rate.
factors.mechanism: causal: long yields set 10y+ fixed mortgage pricing
top_metrics: 10y fixed Dutch mortgage rate band 3.6-4.3% (NHG vs non-NHG), DNB Mar 2026; ECB 2.25% Jun 2026.
Long rates track bond/swap markets, not only ECB policy.

OHAO: 10y fixed ~3.6-4.3%; long rates may rise slightly with bond yields

factors.label: Mortgage interest deduction (hypotheekrenteaftrek)
factors.summary: Major NL-specific tax subsidy that lowers the effective cost of mortgage debt; deduction capped at the basic income-tax bracket and gradually narrowed.
factors.mechanism: causal: HRA raises effective affordability / supports prices
top_metrics: Lowers effective mortgage cost; deduction capped at the basic bracket; 2011 scale ~EUR14bn (~2.5% GDP).
Interacts with borrowing capacity and the rate channel.
